issue_title: Stop backporting fixes / features for unsupported versions (e.g. 1.6)
issue_body:
Currently, Django 1.6 has reached end of life.

Should we not create a Django 1.6 branch of CleanerVersion and let it rest in peace?

This would also mean:
* documenting what version of CleanerVersion works with what release of Django
* removing 1.6 dependant code from CleanerVersion to ease development going further
* removing 1.6 from the Travis test environments

Soon enough (early 2016) Django 1.7 will reach end of life; 

I'm suggesting this to avoid, as much as possible, ``if VERSION >=`` clauses littering the code and letting code take advantage of new Django APIs without worrying about unsupported Django versions.

issue_title: Small bug in writeLP, easy to fix -- Andres Sommerhoff
issue_body:
Hi Stu, just found a small bug in writeLP() of file pulp.py.

In line 1501 dummyVar is used to remember that a dummy variable was necessary to fix the objective affine expression. If there was no need to fix the objective then dummyVar is set to None. Later on, in line 1570 the use of the dummyVar is reverted (by subtracting dummyVar from objective if dummyVar was not None).  The problem is that the line 1512 uses the same "dummyVar" name but for solving a constrain issue. This means that Objective's dummyVar will be overwritten when constrains need to be fixed, making impossible to recover previous objective in line 1570 (actually the objective will end with a new dummy LPvariable included on it). 

The fix is easy. For example, just to rename "dummyVar" to "ObjectiveDummyVar" in line 1501 and line 1570. I will appreciate you can fix this, to avoid some workaround I have done today.

Best regards, Andres

1501         wasNone, dummyVar = self.fixObjective()   
1502         objName = self.objective.name 
1503         if not objName: objName = "OBJ" 
1504         f.write(self.objective.asCplexLpAffineExpression(objName, constant = 0)) 
1505         f.write("Subject To\n") 
1506         ks = list(self.constraints.keys()) 
1507         ks.sort() 
1508         for k in ks: 
1509             constraint = self.constraints[k] 
1510             if not list(constraint.keys()): 
1511                 #empty constraint add the dummyVar 
1512                 dummyVar = self.get_dummyVar() 
1513                 constraint += dummyVar 
1514                 #set this dummyvar to zero so infeasible problems are not made feasible 
1515                 f.write((dummyVar == 0.0).asCplexLpConstraint("_dummy"))
.....
1568         f.write("End\n") 
1569         f.close() 
1570         self.restoreObjective(wasNone, dummyVar)

issue_title: Docs: clarify AWS instructions on upgrading
issue_body:
In the [Upgrading Deis](http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/managing_deis/upgrading-deis/) page, it has this to say about AWS:

> On AWS, Deis enables the PROXY protocol by default. If an in-place upgrade is required, run deisctl config router set proxyProtocol=1, enable PROXY protocol for ports 80 and 443 on the ELB, add a TCP 443:443 listener, and change existing targets and health checks from HTTP to TCP.

I think it should be clarified a little further.

- Do you really need to enable PROXY protocol? Shouldn't this have already been enabled for you when you first installed Deis? If not, how do you enable it? As far as I know, it's not a straight-forward process; there aren't controls for this in the AWS web console.

- `deisctl config router set proxyProtocol=1` probably needs to be ran *before* the uprgade, so this note should be higher up the page.

- It says to _"change existing targets and health checks from HTTP to TCP."_, but not exactly what to configure here (like what port.

I almost messed up an upgrade here because ELB thought my nodes are dead; I changed the health check to TCP and it thought my nodes are dead, haha.

For reference, it looks like this in the AWS web console:

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/74385/15188135/da60da3c-17d6-11e6-888f-f0f354cfd5c3.png)

issue_title: Make testsuite more robust to ConnectionErrors
issue_body:
When we run the testsuite on Travis and AppVeyor, the build matrix is a decent size, and the total number of tests run starts to reach into the 1K order of magnitude.
Very often, with this many tests running, we'll see at least one ConnectionError, but it's just a bit of network flap.

To make the testsuite more robust, we just need to do some retries, and a potentially easy way is to clean and restart tests that hit connection errors. In any case, we need to handle these conditions a bit better than we currently do.

issue_title: Missing Quotes around Dickey Fuller attr parameter
issue_body:
Oh no, you encountered a problem while using *tsfesh*.

We, the maintainers, are happy to help you. When opening an issue, please provide the following information to us:

1. Your operating system
macOS 10.12.6
2. The version of *tsfresh* that you are using
0.10.0
3. The data on which the problem occurred (please do not upload 1000s of time series but try to boil the problem down to a small group or even a singular one)
UCI har data 
4. A minimal code snippet which reproduces the problem/bug
On _calculate_relevance_table_, it returns a dataframe containing a column of the feature calculators with parameters. Some features accept string parameters and _calculate_relevance_table_ returns them surrounded in double quotes: 
> z_earth_user__agg_linear_trend__f_agg_"var"__chunk_len_50__attr_"slope"

however for the _augmented_dickey_fuller_ calcuator, the string param is not surrounded by quotes

> z_earth_user__augmented_dickey_fuller__attr_pvalue

the quotes are necessary for _get_config_from_string_. If the string param doesn't have the double quotes it will raise a _ValueError: malformed string_ on _ast.iteral_eval(value)_

> File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/tsfresh/feature_extraction/settings.py", line 65, in from_columns 
>    config = get_config_from_string(parts) 
>  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/tsfresh/utilities/string_manipulation.py", line 37, in get_config_from_string 
>    dict_if_configs[key] = ast.literal_eval(value) 
>  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/ast.py", line 80, in literal_eval 
>    return _convert(node_or_string) 
>  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/ast.py", line 79, in _convert 
>    raise ValueError('malformed string') 
> ValueError: malformed string
> 


For questions, you can also use our [gitter chatroom](https://gitter.im/tsfresh/)

issue_title: Use Circle-CI parallelism
issue_body: Circle CI will allow us to condense our tests down into a much smaller number of runs using parallelism.  We can setup a much smaller number of jobs, and instead setup the steps in a single job to be all of the different CI runs we need.  Circle lets us define that those steps can be run in parallel and will parallelize them.  With this approach we should be able to cut off a significant amount of overhead to our test runs.

issue_title: conda env update leads to broken environments
issue_body:
A very simple reproduction:

Create the following `environment.yml` file in a directory of your choice:

```
name: condaenvbug
dependencies:
  - python
  - vtk =5.10.1
```

> conda env create
> activate condaenvbug

Change `environment.yml` by bumping VTK to a different version:

```
name: condaenvbug
dependencies:
  - python
  - vtk =6.3.0
```

> (condaenvbug) conda env update
> (condaenvbug) conda list

```
(condaenvbug) C:\Users\Korijn\dev\condabug>conda list
# packages in environment at C:\Users\Korijn\Miniconda3\envs\condaenvbug:
#
pip                       8.1.2                    py27_0    defaults
python                    2.7.12                        0    defaults
setuptools                27.2.0                   py27_1    defaults
vs2008_runtime            9.00.30729.1                  2    defaults
vtk                       5.10.1                   py27_0    defaults
vtk                       6.3.0                    py27_1    defaults
wheel                     0.29.0                   py27_0    defaults
```

As you can see there are now two installed VTK packages. In our bigger projects this simply destroys the environment and makes it unusable. We have fallen back to always calling `conda env remove -n {myenv} -y && conda env create` for now.

_This is not specific to the VTK package, it was just the first one that came to mind for this example._

issue_title: Allow activating multiple environments
issue_body:
Hi,
I'm trying to configure conda to use it on a computing cluster with command line access for multiple users (~300 accounts).
The idea is that we will provide to users some preinstalled conda environments that they can use as they need.

Some people will want to activate several of these environments to perform their compute task. The problem is that the `activate` script calls `deactivate` before doing the activation job, so users can only have 1 environment loaded.

I know that activating multiple environments could be a problem as some incompatibility could arise depending on the packages installed in each env. But in our case, in many situations, it would work perfectly well (and if it's not, users would be able to take care of it).

So I'm wondering if you think this feature could be added to conda somehow? Maybe with a special option for the activate script, and a modification to deactivate to allow it to deactivate *all* the loaded env.
I can work on a PR if you are ok with the idea.

The other option would be to force them to adopt the proper conda way (they create their own environments), but it would make the transition too hard for most of them...

issue_title: Fallback procedure to add to conda's repertoire
issue_body:
If a package is pip-installable, why would it be unavailable on conda? For example: **boto3** which is slowly replacing _boto_ heavily used for Amazon AWS. 

Currently conda suggests a search on anaconda.org -- however, to use an offered package from a random user poses security risk for code such as boto3 which transmits credentials. 

What would the difficulty be in having conda call pip internally for package installation, so that the client does not have to set up a virtualenv environment. Also it is not clear whether one should use pip within a conda environment without breaking its infrastructure. 

Once explained, I would be most happy to update the documentation, or modify the exception case when a package is not within conda's reach. This must be a frustration or FAQ for many.

issue_title: weave: No module named lib.six
issue_body:
I am trying to set up Travis-CI for a library which includes legacy code dependent on [`weave`](https://github.com/scipy/weave). On my local system with a "native" Python installation, the test suite runs just fine. But when run inside a `conda` environment, at each call of `weave.inline()` the following error appears:
```
File "/home/travis/miniconda/envs/test-env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/weave/_dumbdbm_patched.py", line 27, in <module>
    from scipy.lib.six import builtins
ImportError: No module named lib.six
```
This result is unaffected by whether `six` is installed or not.

I am aware that `weave` is deprecated and is not packaged within `conda`. But maybe a small patch of the `scipy` package could solve the problem?

[Travis log](https://travis-ci.org/pik-copan/pyunicorn/jobs/204413323)

Possibly relevant links:
- https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/python-scipy/+bug/1533634
- https://github.com/scipy/weave/pull/3

Versions:
- conda 4.3.13
- python 2.7.13-0
- scipy: 0.17.1
- weave 0.15.0

issue_title: Source of truth: which packages are installed?
issue_body:
What is the source of "truth" as to whether or not Conda is installed?  Conda is installed, but it doesn't seem to know about it, consequently I can't `update`.

```
(root) ➜  ~ which conda
/Users/ijstokes/anaconda/bin/conda

(root) ➜  ~ conda update conda
Error: Package 'conda' is not installed in /Users/ijstokes/anaconda

(root) ➜  ~ conda -V
conda 4.1.3

(root) ➜  ~ /Users/ijstokes/anaconda/bin/conda -V
conda 4.1.3

(root) ➜  ~ /Users/ijstokes/anaconda/bin/conda update conda
Error: Package 'conda' is not installed in /Users/ijstokes/anaconda
```

I think what has happened here for me is that some `conda` operation obliterated `conda` from my root environment and I recovered using the technique I described in #2911.  And from the looks of it, there is some missing information so I can no longer `conda update conda`.

FWIW, I can `conda install conda`, which perhaps is the solution for my immediate problem, but I'd like to know how to refine my "rescue" technique.

issue_title: Revert interfaces for key-value features
`conda install -f` implies `--no-deps` which is a problem for meta-packages
issue_body:
I believe this is related to #446 and #1255, but perhaps not.

First, it is unclear to me *why* the `-f` option to `conda install` should imply `--no-deps`.  Certainly I would say that most people would not expect this to be the case.

But that issue aside, it is a problem for meta-packages which *only* consist of dependencies.  If I do `conda install -f jupyter` then I want to force-install all the dependency packages specified the jupyter meta-package.  More than "I want", in fact "I expect" that is what will happen.  But it doesn't.  And I would suggest that the average user is not going to pick up on the visual hints that force-install of meta-packages do not install the dependencies.  They will just be confused that it hasn't happened or oblivious that it hasn't happened.

issue_title: missing windows magic
issue_body:
I gave `conda` a try for testing on windows.  I found that with the install of miniconda 3.10.1 (64-bit), there were a few things that had to be manually patched so stuff worked as expected.

If you want to run python from the command line:
* associated .py file with conda's python.exe

Not sure if `conda` could or should assume that's what you want.  I think probably.

The bigger issue is that there was some command line magic missing from the registry keys.
These two registry entries needed editing with regedit:
* HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT\Applications\python27.exe\shell\open\command
* HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT\py_auto_file\shell\open\command

Each key needed a `%*` to be added to the end of the existing command string -- otherwise conda python ignores all but the first command line argument.

issue_title: Allow multiple arguments to read_callback function in count_coverage
issue_body:
I'm using the pysam function `count_coverage`, described [here](http://pysam.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#pysam.AlignmentFile.count_coverage), to compute per-site read depth from a bamfile. Within `count_coverage`, I'm using `read_callback` to specify my own user-defined function for filtering out reads that fail to meet a series of defined conditions. However, the read_callback function can only take one argument, so all other arguments must be global variables. 

It would be great to be able to specify multiple arguments to the read_callback function, instead of using global variables. Is this possible?

This is what I currently have:
```
# read_callback function
def keep_read(aln):
   if np.mean(aln.query_qualities) < min_readq:
      return False
   elif aln.mapping_quality < min_mapq:
      return False
   else:
      return True

# global arguments to read_callback function
min_readq=20
min_mapq=30

# count coverage
with pysam.AlignmentFile(bampath, 'rb') as bamfile:
   counts = bamfile.count_coverage(chrom, start, end, read_callback=keep_read)
```

This is what I'd like:
```
# read_callback function
def keep_read(aln, min_readq, min_mapq):
   if np.mean(aln.query_qualities) < min_readq:
      return False
   elif aln.mapping_quality < min_mapq:
      return False
   else:
      return True

# count coverage
with pysam.AlignmentFile(bampath, 'rb') as bamfile:
   counts = bamfile.count_coverage(chrom, start, end, read_callback=keep_read, args=[20, 30])
```

Thanks,
Stephen

issue_title: Pysam hangs on remote BAM fetch when server doesn't implement byte serving
issue_body:
Opening a remote BAM file causes pysam to hang when the BAM file is being served by a server that doesn't support byte serving.

To reproduce, run a server that doesn't implement byte serving in one terminal:
```
$ ls
HG00096.mapped.ILLUMINA.bwa.GBR.low_coverage.20120522.bam
HG00096.mapped.ILLUMINA.bwa.GBR.low_coverage.20120522.bam.bai
$ python -m SimpleHTTPServer 8080
Serving HTTP on 0.0.0.0 port 8080 ...
```

And in another terminal, try and fetch the file:
```
>>> import pysam
>>> a = 'http://localhost:8080/HG00096.mapped.ILLUMINA.bwa.GBR.low_coverage.20120522.bam'
>>> b = pysam.AlignmentFile(a)
[knet_seek] SEEK_END is not supported for HTTP. Offset is unchanged.
```
The pysam call will not return.

issue_title: Comment/Uncomment should use indicator column in fixed-form reference-format

issue_title: Enhance indices.exists_alias() behaviour
issue_body:
Currently if you call `indices.exists_alias(index=indexname)`  without giving any value for the parameter `name`, it will return `False` even if the index has one or several aliases. According to the HTTP API this behaviour is wrong, such a call should return `True`. 
That could be simply corrected either by adding a condition evaluating the name before making the request or by setting the default value of name at an empty string.

issue_title: test_elasticsearch/start_elasticsearch.sh needs to be run from test_elasticsearch
issue_body:
In the command line, there is:

    -v `pwd`/../elasticsearch:/code/elasticsearch

So if it's launched directly from the git clone directory, it resolve to a wrong directory.

To resolve it correctly, the following code would be better:
```
CODE_DIR=$(cd $(dirname $0)/.. && pwd)/elasticsearch
```

issue_body:
From https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/issues/1384#issuecomment-348492911 and @charliejllewellyn

```python
In my scenario we are querying the metadata endpoint which calls a method (_populate_from_metadata_endpoint) in msrestazure/azure_cloud.py. This appears to be hardcoded to not pass verify=False to retrieve the endpoints. Editing this to pass this verify=False gets Ansible running.
```

This endpoint does not required authentication, so it's a standalone call and `requests` parameters should be available (or at least verify).

Okay, so 2.84 introduced API breaking changes with https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/commit/626a913866158e5956cfa2ae7f5f6350e99d783e

However, `BT_BULLET_VERSION` was already bumped up to 284 at [2.83.6](https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/blob/2.83.6/src/LinearMath/btScalar.h) (see [2.83.5](https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/blob/2.83.5/src/LinearMath/btScalar.h) where it still was 283)
To my surprise this meant [2.84](https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/blob/2.84/src/LinearMath/btScalar.h) is actually.. *drumroll* 285

- Is that intended? (It broke our codebase because we added checks for 284 because we wanted to catch ... bullet 2.84)
- How to best check for API feature level?
- How to check for a printable version number?
- Will this go back to normal?

issue_title: 2.0 dev release
_CoroGuard cannot be awaited
issue_body:
## Long story short

1) [_CoroGuard](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/aiohttp/helpers.py#L65) class has no implementation for `__await__` method, only `__iter__`. 
That's why`_CoroGuard` instance cannot be `awaited`, just `yielded from`.

2) According to warning [message](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/aiohttp/client.py#L538), I can assume that `ClientSession.close` method is not supposed to be awaited. 
Thus, 
- `_CoroGuard` should check that it was not awaited(remove `not` from `if not self._awaited` in `__del__` method,
**BUT** 
- `_CoroGuard` is supposed to check that coroutine actually was awaited. 
Those two statements contradict each other.
Currently, `_CoroGuard` is used only for `ClientSession.close`.

## Expected behaviour

- `_CoroGuard` can be awaited
-  warn `DeprecationWarning` if `ClientSession.close` method is awaited or yielded from(not the opposite, as it is now)

## Actual behaviour

- `_CoroGuard` cannot be awaited
- Warns `DeprecationWarning` if `ClientSession.close` method is not awaited or not yielded from.

## Steps to reproduce

```
#!/usr/bin/env python
import aiohttp
import asyncio


async def coro_function(loop):
    session = aiohttp.ClientSession(loop=loop)
    await session.close()


if __name__ == '__main__':
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    try:
        loop.run_until_complete(coro_function(loop))
    finally:
        loop.close()
```

## Your environment
```
OS X 10.12.3
python 3.6.0
aiohttp==2.2.0
```

issue_title: Error fails silently for `asyncio tasks`
issue_body:
Using the notebook, an exception is expected for the following code:

```python
import asyncio
from datetime import datetime

# coroutine function
async def anything(i):
    print(i, datetime.now())
    await asyncio.sleep(i)
    err                                                    # exceptions seem to be ignored

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
tasks = [loop.create_task(anything(i)) for i in range(1, 7)]
loop.run_until_complete(asyncio.wait(tasks)) 
loop.close()
print('F', datetime.now())
```
However, no exception is raised after the cell is executed. As far as I have tested, expected errors in coroutines are silenced for several implementations of asyncio tasks.

I have confirmed in console ipython on two machines that the latter code also runs without errors, **but actually generates the following traceback only *after quitting* ipython.**

```bash
In [2]: 
Do you really want to exit ([y]/n)? 
Task exception was never retrieved
future: <Task finished coro=<anything() done, defined at <ipython-input-1-fd581dbf29e1>:5> exception=NameError("name 'err' is not defined",)>
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/asyncio/tasks.py", line 239, in _step
    result = coro.send(None)
  File "<ipython-input-1-fd581dbf29e1>", line 8, in anything
    err                                                    # exceptions seem to be ignored
NameError: name 'err' is not defined
```

This implicit exception behavior is strange, and  I believe is possibly a BUG.  Exceptions should be raised in explicitly in the notebook and console. 

Windows 7 | 64-bit | Python 3.5.1 | IPython 4.1.2 | notebook 4.2.0

issue_title: A filestore by any other name would smell as sweet?
issue_body:
It's exhausting to explain that "filestore" does not store files. I like "asset_registry" (better than "resource_registry", the current name of the module in databroker). But I'm questioning whether we want to go through the painful API break of changing not only the module name but also the object names everywhere. On balance, I'm ambivalent.

Where do others stand on this?

issue_title: ToilJenkinsSlave: SLURM setup is sensitive to instance type
issue_body:
[Changing](https://github.com/BD2KGenomics/toil-scripts/pull/251#issuecomment-214912601) the instance type of a toil-jenkins-slave to a type with less memory causes Toil's SLURM tests to hang.

I believe this is due to the fact that the available memory is evaluated at `cgcloud create` time and the baked into the config file and hence the image. The memory should be evaluated every time the machine starts up.

issue_title: Howto handle hook changes
issue_body:
I have a hook which needs to be changed. In essence it needs an additional argument.

The problem is, that plugins should be able to use the new argument, but still work on older versions where the argument didn't exist yet.

If I add the argument and use it in the hook implementation in the plugin, then the plugin doesn't work with older versions anymore: ``Argument(s) ... are declared in the hookimpl but can not be found in the hookspec``

Using kwargs with defaults also doesn't work, the argument isn't passed then.

If I add a new hook, then both my implementations in the plugin are called, because the old hook still needs to be called for older plugins.

One idea would be the possibility to mark a hook specification deprecated with the name of the replacement and then when loading a plugin and it implements both hooks forget about the old hook. If it only supports the old hook, then add a warning about the deprecation.

To reproduce, run any suite of tests with less than the desired line coverage. [Sample traceback](https://gist.github.com/justanr/3cbc4dcb136638a2215b#file-traceback)

I only noticed this because I'm starting a new project with one test to see if I could get a combined HTML coverage report for all environments.

I'm wondering if [this line](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-cov/blob/ece248242b21786c241a47f24c7a8f8f0976e735/src/pytest_cov/plugin.py#L175) should be a `sys.exit(1)` (or similar) instead

issue_title: Report is generated and written to disk regardless of selenium_capture_debug
issue_body:
In pytest.ini I have:

selenium_capture_debug = failure

However, pytest-html still generates a report if all tests pass. Same goes for if I set it to 'never'.

Now, in pytest-selenium#pytest_runtest_makereport(), it works as expected: the item.config.hook.pytest_selenium_capture_debug() is never called.

But, in plugin.py (pytest-html), pytest_runtest_logreport() and pytest_terminal_summary() are still called. I cannot find any checks for the ini config in pytest-html.

It all used to work as expected.

Versions:
pytest==3.0.5
pytest-selenium==1.6 (we can't run newer due to Selenium 3 requirement)
pytest-html==1.12.0

issue_title: The reason behind disabling the plugin by default
issue_body:
Please see my description of my previous problem [here](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/issues/2992).

I wanted to ask why do we have to explicitly enable this plugin? Based on what's written [here](https://docs.pytest.org/en/2.7.3/plugins.html?highlight=re#external-plugins):

```If a plugin is installed, pytest automatically finds and integrates it, there is no need to activate it. ```

But having to activate this plugin separately goes against this.

issue_title: atcab_write_pubkey() fails
issue_body:
Hello,

I'm trying to store a 64-byte long ECC public key in slot 8 without any success. I get this error ATCA_EXECUTION_ERROR
Maybe the settings for the slot are not correct. I use these values to configure the slot:

issue_title: add XmlValidationFile endpoint to ASM policy
Product attribute's code generated with dashes
issue_body:
Let's see in the following code:
```python
>>> class Tmp(object):
...     pass
...     
>>> tmp = Tmp()
>>> setattr(tmp, 'temp-code', 123)
>>> tmp.temp-code
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<input>", line 1, in <module>
AttributeError: 'Tmp' object has no attribute 'temp'
>>> tmp.__dict__
{'temp-code': 123}
>>> getattr(tmp, 'temp-code')
123
```
Attribute was set, object have this attribute, but this attribute can not be accessed directly as `tmp.temp-code`. That's right, this is Python.
Product's attributes with spaces in their names have generated code with dashes. So, these attributes can not be accessed with `product.attr.some-attribute`, because of dash in attribute's codename is interpreted as minus sign in program's code. Documentation for `ProductAttributesContainer` offer this way to get attribute's values, but one way to get value is `getattr(product.attr, 'some-attribute')`.
This behavior can be fixed with using AutoSlugField with underscore as separator [here](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/master/src%2Foscar%2Fapps%2Fcatalogue%2Fabstract_models.py#L780-L785):
```python
class AbstractProductAttribute(models.Model):
    ...
    code = models.SlugField(
        _('Code'), max_length=128,
        validators=[RegexValidator(
            regex=r'^[a-zA-Z\-_][0-9a-zA-Z\-_]*$',
            message=_("Code can only contain the letters a-z, A-Z, digits, "
                      "minus and underscores, and can't start with a digit"))])
    ...
```
Any thoughts?

issue_title: Apache2 Auto-install issues with WSGI server modules...
issue_body:
I attempted to use the letsencrypt-auto script to install a cert on one of my domains that is using WSGI modules on Apache2.  The problem is that when it copies the base .conf file to generate one for the SSL equivalent, it doesn't generate new WSGIDaemonProcess names.

The error you get is as follows:
> Error while running apache2ctl configtest.
> Action 'configtest' failed.
> The Apache error log may have more information.
> AH00526: Syntax error on line 36 of 
> /etc/apache2/sites-enabled/mysite.conf:
> Name duplicates previous WSGI daemon definition.

The Apache log file doesn't have any additional details, but the problem is the following line in the conf files:

```
        WSGIDaemonProcess mydaemon \
            processes=2 \
            threads=1 \
            display-name=%{GROUP} \
            python-path=/var/www/myserver/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages \
            python-eggs=/var/www/myserver/data/python-egg-cache

        WSGIProcessGroup mydaemon
```

The WSGI Daemon Process name must be unique and there's already one in the base mysite.conf file and so the mysite.conf-le-ssl.conf (or whatever it gets called) causes a duplicate name.

I don't know that there's an easy fix for the auto script because I believe it's going to necessitate having two different daemons running -- one for the SSL version and one for unencrypted.  If the user is switching to encrypted only where there's only one daemon running, perhaps it would work, if the unencrypted gets dropped or delegated to the encrypted.

Otherwise, where it will get tricky is whether or not two daemons will play nicely when run from the same setup (i.e. same files).  Now in my case, as evidenced above, it's a Python script and it happens to interface with a mysql engine on the backend, etc.  The mysql daemon should sort out database accesses, but it may be desired (or even necessary) to have separate caches and scripts, etc, between the two for the configuration files and things that would otherwise be co-accessed, as the daemon may not be expecting to have to do file locking and such.

I had a similar issue when setting up my development environment on this server, though that is a completely separate code base with different paths, etc, so I just named it something like "mydaemon-dev" and ran different daemons.  But letsencrypt-auto probably won't know if two daemons can run simultaneously with the same configuration or not, and so it probably can't just change the name to mydaemon-ssl or something for the copy and expect it to work.

The simple solution is for the user to manually add the SSL setup to the server, but what this bug report is about is for adding a detection mechanism to the letencrypt-auto script to see if a WSGIDaemonProcess is defined and properly notify the user that it has to be manually enabled rather than spewing out a bunch of warnings and exiting in a somewhat incomplete state (as I don't think it finished setting up my other virtual domains that otherwise had no issues until I ran it again and excluded this domain).

Thanks!
Donna

issue_title: Process assignment events without consuming messages
issue_body:
Description
===========
I have a peculiar consumer group setup in which I need to **process** the assign/revoke **callbacks without consuming messages** necessarily.

In my application there are messages generated by different producers that belong to their respected priority group. There are huge bursts of new messages and the consumers have limited resources, so it is not possible to process all messages in real time. It would be enough to have the higher priority messages block the lower tiers until there are none left. To achieve this priority message routing, I make consumers for each priority group (topic) and read the messages from the highest priority consumer possible.

My problem arises when there are mulitple of these router processes. Each of them knows how much messages the consumers can receive in total in a minute, but they must know how many messages they need to produce **individually**. Here's a simple equation:
`number_of_messages_current_router_process_should_produce = number_of_messages_consumers_can_process_in_total * (partitions_assigned_to_current_router_process / total_number_of_partitions_of_router_processes)`

Since the number of partitions for the given topics are mostly static and there is no metadata fetching for it (yet) in this kafka python library, I get that from configuration which is mostly fine.

I have set up the consumers with on_assign and on_revoke callbacks to keep track of how many partitions I have, but those only update when poll() is called. However, since I do not want to consume messages from the lower priority consumers those will not update their assignment for extended periods of time. Overall the router processes end up producing half of what they should because the assignment of the lower priority consumers do not get processed. (even though they are actually assigned in the background)

How to reproduce
================
There is not much to be reproduced as the documentation clearly states that callbacks are processed during poll(). There is a new consume() method which also processes events but passing num_messages=0 to it will make it do nothing.

I've looked around a little in librdkafka and found that rd_kafka_poll() could do just what I want, but confluent-kafka-python sets up the consumers with rd_kafka_poll_set_consumer() that will redirect the main kafka events to the consumer's queue. In this setup the rd_kafka_poll() is not usable according to the documentation.

Even if another setup would be used the event list for rd_kafka_poll() does not include assignment events in the documentation. I'm guessing that could be because those events are tied to the consumer all the time, but I'm not sure.

In the end my question is:
**Is there a way or could there be a way to process the assignment events of a consumer without consuming any messages?**

Checklist
=========

 - [ ] confluent-kafka-python and librdkafka version (`confluent_kafka.version()` and `confluent_kafka.libversion()`): ('0.11.4', 721920), ('0.11.4', 722175)

issue_title: Client segfaults when threads are used
issue_body:
As part of https://github.com/confluentinc/confluent-kafka-python/pull/40, we started noticing that our tests were failing with a segfault in the Travis CI container, but only for Python 3.4.

We were unable to reproduce this locally on our laptops, but with Docker, and using a Travis CI container, we can. Here are the steps to trigger a segfault:

    # Start Docker
    docker run -it quay.io/travisci/travis-python /bin/bash

Once you're in your Docker container, run these commands:

```
sudo add-apt-repository ppa:fkrull/deadsnakes
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install python3.4
pip install pip==7.1.0
pip install virtualenv==13.1.2
virtualenv -p /usr/bin/python3.4 /test
source /test/bin/activate
cd ~
git clone --depth=50 https://github.com/confluentinc/confluent-kafka-python.git
cd confluent-kafka-python/
git fetch origin pull/40/head:40
git checkout 40
export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=$PWD/tmp-build/lib
bash tools/bootstrap-librdkafka.sh v0.9.2 tmp-build
pip install pytest-timeout
pip install -v --global-option=build_ext --global-option="-Itmp-build/include/:/opt/python/3.4.2/include/python3.4m" --global-option="-Ltmp-build/lib" . .[avro]
git checkout 8148a79026d7324c34a24a03ba9043ebe2f931fd
py.test -v --timeout 20 --ignore=tmp-build --import-mode append
```

This will show a segfault before the tests finish.

Furthermore, I'm able to trigger this on `master` without any of our changes. After the above commands, run:

```
git checkout master
```

Then apply this diff:

```
diff --git a/tests/test_threads.py b/tests/test_threads.py
index 09f6467..e4f76e8 100644
--- a/tests/test_threads.py
+++ b/tests/test_threads.py
@@ -64,6 +64,35 @@ def test_thread_safety():
 
     print('Done')
 
+import time
+class myThread (threading.Thread):
+    def __init__(self, threadID, name, counter):
+        threading.Thread.__init__(self)
+        self.threadID = threadID
+        self.name = name
+        self.counter = counter
+    def run(self):
+        print("Starting " + self.name)
+        print_time(self.name, self.counter, 5)
+        print("Exiting " + self.name)
+
+def print_time(threadName, delay, counter):
+    while counter:
+        if exitFlag:
+            threadName.exit()
+        time.sleep(delay)
+        print("%s: %s" % (threadName, time.ctime(time.time())))
+        counter -= 1
+
+def test_foo():
+    # Create new threads
+    thread1 = myThread(1, "Thread-1", 1)
+    thread2 = myThread(2, "Thread-2", 2)
+
+    # Start new Threads
+    thread1.start()
+    thread2.start()
+
```

It's just some example of Python threading that I found on Stack Overflow. After the diff is applied, run the same py.test command again. It will again segfault.

issue_title: Cut down on Appveyor builds
issue_body:
Currently this is the Appveyor build matrix:

```yaml
  matrix:
    - PYTHON: "C:/Python27"
    - PYTHON: "C:/Python27-x64"
    - PYTHON: "C:/Python33"
    - PYTHON: "C:/Python33-x64"
    - PYTHON: "C:/Python34"
    - PYTHON: "C:/Python34-x64"
    - PYTHON: "C:/Python35"
    - PYTHON: "C:/Python35-x64"
    - PYTHON: "C:/Python36"
    - PYTHON: "C:/Python36-x64"
```

I'm not sure I can recall a time when we had a failure on x64 that didn't also fail on x86, and Appveyor is often the slow step in the CI (I think they don't do concurrent builds). I'm thinking we should probably cut down and re-organize the build matrix, like this:

```yaml
  matrix:
    - PYTHON: "C:/Python27-x64"
    - PYTHON: "C:/Python36-x64"

    - PYTHON: "C:/Python27"
    - PYTHON: "C:/Python33"
    - PYTHON: "C:/Python34"
    - PYTHON: "C:/Python35"
    - PYTHON: "C:/Python36"
```

We'd be testing on all supported versions (except 3.2, which we've droped from these builds for whatever reason ages ago) on x86, and only test the latest two builds on x64, but the latest two x64 builds would be executed *first*.

The other option is to test x64 on all versions and only test the latest two with x86. The downside there is that I think 1. x86 is more likely to have problems than x64 and 2. you're *more likely* to be using x86 if you're also using an old version of Python.

I imagine when we add a binary component, we'll want to turn the full matrix back on again at least temporarily, since that's way more likely to have architecture + version corner cases.

issue_title: datetime.timedelta cannot be added to relativedelta
issue_body:
```python
from dateutil import relativedelta
import datetime

relativedelta.relativedelta(years=1) + datetime.timedelta(days=1)
```

Results in a `TypeError: unsupported type for add operation`, despite both being time offsets.

However, it is perfectly possible to add a `relativedelta` to another `relativedelta`:

```python

relativedelta.relativedelta(years=1) + relativedelta.relativedelta(years=1)
# relativedelta(years=+2)
```

I'm happy to submit a PR if there is interest (beyond myself) in this.

issue_title: test server configrution and breaking signin to two seperate step
Better handling of ack requests
issue_body:
Sometimes at any random point in time, an `ack` request may be received, thus considering the invoked method to be confirmed. This will end the loop that checks whether the result was received yet or not, thus having a `None` result and failing at random points on the code.

The problem however is that there are some requests which do depend on the `ack` request to confirm the invoked method, such as the `LogoutRequest`. How to tell which should be confirmed and which shouldn't doesn't seem trivial, furthermore, this happens randomly (although it is more likely to happen if sending several `GetFileRequest`'s, personal experience).

Maybe one should look into the `tdesktop` source to check how they handle this kind of stuff.

issue_title: non-scatter fields in scatter workflow steps not working

issue_title: Bug in normalizing ident var that is written as delins
issue_body:
When I was normalizing the clinvar variants, I found the variant

> NC_000003.12:g.46709584_46709610del27insAAGAAGAAGAAGAAGAAGAAGAAGAAG

issue_title: fields.Method result should be able to be wrapped into other field types
Support for object type hierarchies
issue_body:
Consider the following use case: you have a hierarchy of object types each of which can have unique attributes and you want to be able to serialize all them in a generic manner. E.g. you have type Event with subtypes representing particular type of event and having attributes specific to that type of event. I want to return a list of all events thus this list can contain events of different types. I would like to create a schema per event type (the same way I create a subclass per event type) and have a union-like Schema that will encode event type in some attribute on serialization and will use this field to pick particular schema on deserialization. This can also be extended to types that are do not have common predecessor.

```python
class FooSchema(marshmallow.Schema):
    foo = marshmallow.fields.String(required=True)

class BarSchema(Schema):
    bar = marshmallow.fields.Integer(required=True)
```
What I would like to do is to have mixed list of objects which schemas are above and have them serialize uniformly.

```python
MyUberSchema().dump([Foo(foo='hello'), Bar(bar=123)], many=True).data
# => [{'type': 'foo', 'foo': 'hello'}, {'type': 'bar', 'bar': 123}]
```
Is there an existing way to do that and/or is this a valid use case ?

issue_title: dump_only whitelist/inverse / load_only whilst including when dumping
issue_body:
I feel like this kind of touches on #375, but that didn't seem to align with the behaviour that I think many would find useful. Also highly related to #61 

It would be useful to use the meta class (or constructor) to be able to specify a set of fields that are whitelisted for deserialisation. If the field doesn't exist in the iterable, it cannot be deserialised. 

If a user of marshmallow had a large schema class, with many fields, but only wanted 1 of those fields to be deserializable/writable, from my understanding they could either:
- name every field they want as read only in `dump_only` attribute (lots of fields, prone to errors, ie missing a field)
- When loading data, specify `only=('fieldname',)` upon instantiating the schema. (prone to errors, forgetting to put this in when instantiating)

Am I missing something, or is this a shortcoming that could be addressed? 

Also to note, the naming of `load_only` feels like a misnomer without reading the docs in detail - If you specify a field in load_only, it will only be available for loading, not dumping - but taking another perspective to this could imply that it's an iterable of fields that are whitelisted to be loaded, but can still be dumped.

issue_title: Change behavior or Resource._handle_500 method
issue_body:
We ran into a problem with how [Resource._handle_500](https://github.com/toastdriven/django-tastypie/blob/71a1f0a161a7f3d549bce9b07984212ee4a8c81a/tastypie/resources.py#L251) behaves substantially and surprisingly differently when ``DEBUG`` is enabled and when not.

Basically, we have a web application which will hide a lot of 500 errors  (retry with timeout etc.). When ``DEBUG`` is enabled, to our surprise we accidentally found that exceptions in resource handling were not actually visible in the application console or error logs at all.

I think this is bad behavior for two reasons:

* Tastypie effectively silently swallows exceptions (in our case, the response is in JSON format ... reading traceback field isn't that easy -- and you have to actively look for any failures in the browser!), breaking the principle of least suprise
* Behavior regarding logging is different between development (``DEBUG=True``) and production environments (``DEBUG=False``), meaning that some aspects of logging configuration cannot be tested in development verson at all

As a fix I suggest (we did this in a local Resource mix-in):

* Move [logging the exception](https://github.com/toastdriven/django-tastypie/blob/71a1f0a161a7f3d549bce9b07984212ee4a8c81a/tastypie/resources.py#L277) to happen always before [sending DEBUG traceback to the client](https://github.com/toastdriven/django-tastypie/blob/71a1f0a161a7f3d549bce9b07984212ee4a8c81a/tastypie/resources.py#L264)

Although the current behavior was sensible earlier, when mail_admins was called from exception handler, this is not the case anymore. The current mechanism to mail admins is to use logger handler ``django.utils.log.AdminEmailHandler``, and even suppress sending with ``django.utils.log.RequireDebugFalse`` to suppress mailing when ``DEBUG`` is enabled. (The rationale here is of course to allow the use of same or very similar logging configuration in both production and development.)

issue_title: use HDF5 files for large arrays in spa and irradiance
issue_body:
The very large arrays in SPA and irradiance modules take a lot of space, which IMO makes those modules hard to navigate. See #235. Also having them in code can present other issues, for example if those coefficients should be changed or expanded. _EG:_ If new sets of Perez coefficients are released.

Some proposals:
1. Move data to the bottom of the module, and make it constant. When modules are imported, first only the top level symbols are interpreted, so the module attribute `MYDATA` will be interpreted before the class attribute `mydata` and won't raise a `NameError` for an unresolved reference.

``` python
import numpy as np

class ClsUsingData(objects):
    mydata = MYDATA

    def __init__(self, *args):
        # do stuff with data

# other stuff

# all constants with very large arrays at the bottom of module
MYDATA = np.arrary([
    # lots of data
])
```
1. Use HDF5 files using [`h5py`](http://www.h5py.org/). These files are highly optimized for speed and act exactly like NumPy arrays. It's okay to keep them open, they'll be closed when Python exits. HDF5 will quickly load the data only when sliced (using mp threads if h5py built with mpicc or openmp) so memory usage is faster and more efficient. Alternately, copying all of the data out of the file into a numpy array will allow you to close the file, but it is slower and less efficient.

``` python
import h5py
import os
DIRNAME = os.path.dirname(__file__)
MYDATA = os.path.join(DIRNAME, 'mydata.h5')

class ClsUsingData(objects):
    mydata = h5py.File(MYDATA)  # leave it open

# alternately copy the data to a numpy array and close the file:
# h5_data_path = '/group/dataset'
# with h5py.File(MYDATA, 'r') as f:
#     mydata = np.array(f[h5_data_path])

    def __init__(self, *args):
        # do stuff with data
```

issue_title: Duality bug: overflow encountered when expanding vector
issue_body:
I've been tracking down this Heisenbug for a while. It kept plaguing my invocations of Duality over the API, but every time I asked for the smtlib dump of the problematic CHC system and copy-pasted it directly into the smtlib interpreter, the bug would disappear. I finally caught one that fails in the interpreter, and I was able to whittle it down into the following minimal example. For some extra puzzlement, the comments indicate a few transformations one can do that will make the bug vanish.

```
(set-option :fixedpoint.engine duality)

(declare-rel R_1 ())
(declare-rel R_2 (Int Int Int))
(declare-rel R_3 (Int Int Int))

(declare-var A Int)
(declare-var B Int)
(declare-var C Int)
(declare-var D Int)
(declare-var E Int)
(declare-var F Int)

(rule R_1)

(rule (=> (and R_1 ; <-- bug disappears if we eliminate R_1
               (not (= F E)) ; <-- bug disappears if these two vars are in lexicographical order, as far as I can tell (try other names)
               (= D F))
      (R_2 D E F)))

(rule (=> (and (R_2 D E F)
               (or (= 0 D) (= C D))
               (or (= B E) (= C E))
               (or (= B F) (= 0 F))
               (= A 0)) ; <-- bug disappears if we eliminate var A
          (R_3 A B C)))

(query R_3)
```

issue_body:
Another question regarding my use of z3 in the python script symbolic execution engine. In longer examples of the engine, the script spends much of it's time re-creating the z3 solver when it state splits. My current implementation simply instantiates a new solver, and then copies over all the solver.assertions(). Is there any way to copy the solver without having this overhead?

issue_title: ResourceNotFound exception on removetree
issue_body:
`removetree` breaks when (presumably) boto cleans up a directory after all files have been deleted.

To replicate (I am using a local minio server to test against): 

```python 
bucket_name = 'test-bucket1'

from minio import Minio
m = Minio(
    endpoint=MINIO_ENDPOINT, access_key=MINIO_KEY, 
    secret_key=MINIO_SECRET, secure=False
)
m.make_bucket(bucket_name)

from fs_s3fs import S3FS
fs = S3FS(
    bucket_name=bucket_name, aws_access_key_id=MINIO_KEY,
    aws_secret_access_key=MINIO_SECRET, endpoint_url='http://' + MINIO_ENDPOINT
)

dr = fs.makedirs('data/hello')
with dr.openbin('world', 'w') as f:
    f.write(b'somedata')

fs.removetree('data')
```

which raises

```python
ResourceNotFound                          Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-9-8b5db24ca94e> in <module>()
----> 1 fs.removetree('data')

~/anaconda3/envs/dev/lib/python3.6/site-packages/fs/base.py in removetree(self, dir_path)
   1057             for _path, info in gen_info:
   1058                 if info.is_dir:
-> 1059                     self.removedir(_path)
   1060                 else:

~/anaconda3/envs/dev/lib/python3.6/site-packages/fs_s3fs/_s3fs.py in removedir(self, path)
    636         if _path == '/':
    637             raise errors.RemoveRootError()
--> 638         info = self.getinfo(_path)

~/anaconda3/envs/dev/lib/python3.6/site-packages/fs_s3fs/_s3fs.py in getinfo(self, path, namespaces)

ResourceNotFound: resource '/data/hello' not found
```

issue_title: angr looks for Self modifying code even if not asked to.
issue_body:
When loading code through [_load_bytes](https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/master/angr/engines/vex/engine.py#L495) there is a test for self modyfing code. I think the intention was that if asked to consider self modifying code, there were additional sanity checks to be made before really looking for (are memory permissions ok?, ...).

But in the current implementation, the code ignores the value of `self._support_selfmodifying_code` and considers self modifying code whenever possible

```python
        smc = self._support_selfmodifying_code
        if state:
            try:
                p = state.memory.permissions(addr)
                if p.symbolic:
                    smc = True
                else:
                    smc = claripy.is_true(p & 2 != 0)
            except: # pylint: disable=bare-except
                smc = True # I don't know why this would ever happen, we checked this right?
```

I would be rather expecting a test like 
```python
        smc = self._support_selfmodifying_code
        if smc and state:
            try:
                p = state.memory.permissions(addr)
                if p.symbolic:
                    smc = True
                else:
                    smc = claripy.is_true(p & 2 != 0)
            except: # pylint: disable=bare-except
                smc = True # I don't know why this would ever happen, we checked this right?
```

However, this is causing the program to also read the wrong memory area and go off the tracks.

This was started through a script that first set stdin input to start with a bunch of "A" values. Expected output would be that the read would have read in that bunch of "A" values. Yes, i seeked back to 0 :-p

issue_title: Gauss Support is needed for GaudiRun
issue_body:
Support for Gauss and the GausSplitter is needed in GaudiRun.

The original implementation pickles the resulting python file and passes it to `gaudirun.py`. This means an additional level of work is needed to find out what exactly (and in what order) various options are passed to the running Job.

I'm aware of at least 1 minor bug in the original Gauss+GaussSplitter implementation which has been found by a PhD student trying to get their analysis working where there is some minor disagreement between different parts of the code to the number of jobs being generated.

In the original implementation there is 1 pickle file per subjob which means a unique inputsandbox.
I'm keen to explore passing the unique options here as runtime script arguments which would mean that submitting many subjobs should be much quicker.
(This may require reassembling a pickle object on the WN, I don't yet know)

issue_title: 500 when validation fails with an integer value instead of string
issue_body:
The way we transform the validation errors of `jsonschema` to `cornice` is not rock-solid.
It looks like values and fields are sometimes mixed-up.
When an integer reaches the `name` parameter of `raise_invalid()` it fails miserably.

```diff
         except jsonschema_exceptions.ValidationError as e:
             field = e.path.pop() if e.path else e.validator_value.pop()
-            raise_invalid(self.request, name=field, description=e.message)
+            raise_invalid(self.request, name=str(field), description=e.message)
```

issue_title: If-Match: * does not match the spec
issue_body: Per https://github.com/Kinto/kinto/pull/1064#issuecomment-279351031, the behavior of `If-Match: *` is the opposite of `If-None-Match: *`. `If-None-Match: *` should succeed if no resource exists at the given URL and fail if any resource exists at the given URL, but `If-Match: *` should fail if no resource exists at the given URL and succeed if any resource exists at the given URL. Currently `If-Match: *` does nothing (it always succeeds).

def auto_retry(api_call_method):
    """ A decorator to automatically retry 500s (Service Unavailable) and skip 400s (Bad Request) or 404s (Not Found). """
    def call_wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        try:
            return api_call_method(*args, **kwargs)
        except APIError as error:
            # Try Again Once
            if error.error_code in [500]:
                try:
                    print("Got a 500, trying again...")
                    return api_call_method(*args, **kwargs)
                except APIError as another_error:
                    if another_error.error_code in [500, 400, 404]:

                    else:
                        raise another_error

            # Skip

            # Fatal

issue_title: Drop static attributes from ServerConfig class
issue_body:
The `pulp_smash.config.ServerConfig` class currently has static attributes named `_xdg_config_dir` and `_xdg_config_file`. These attributes are used by other methods when those methods need to choose a configuration file to manipulate. For example, `read()` consults these attributes when deciding which file to read.

This works well if there's only one configuration file that is in use. But what if there's multiple configuration files in use? As things stand, one needs to write something like this:

```python
cfg1 = ServerConfig.read(path='~/.config/pulp_smash/config1.json')
cfg2 = ServerConfig.read(path='~/.config/pulp_smash/config2.json')
```

This works well if you know exactly where on the filesystem these files exist. But that assumption is too invasive! One of the aims of the XDG Base Directory Specification is to avoid this level of deep integration with the system. Using hard-coded paths is a Bad Thing. What if the `XDG_CONFIG_DIRS` environment variable has been set? What if system-wide configuration files are being read? And so on?

This issue can be solved by changing `_xdg_config_file` and `_xdg_config_dir` from class to instance attributes. It'll allow us to write code like this:

```python
cfg = ServerConfig()
cfg._xdg_config_file = 'config1'
cfg1 = cfg.read()
cfg._xdg_config_file = 'config2'
cfg1 = cfg.read()
```

This is, frankly, still pretty ugly. I don't like it. But dropping global state is a move in the right direction. We can move forward from there.

issue_title: Numpy floating point precision causes SpaCy vocab KeyValue error
issue_body:
If you have a `token` in your `nlp(text)` whose `token.orth` is a very large int (for example: `886050111519832510`), then in `WMD._common_vocabulary_batch`, `numpy.concatenate((words1, words2))` will approximate those integers in `joint, index = numpy.unique(numpy.concatenate((words1, words2)), return_index=True)`, thereby returning incorrect/invalid vocabulary keys for `evec = self.embeddings[joint]` in `WMD._estimate_WMD_relaxation_batch`.


```python
ipdb> 886050111519832510 in words1
True
ipdb> 886050111519832510 in words2
True
ipdb> 886050111519832510 in numpy.concatenate((words1, words2))
False
```

This last false occurs because 886050111519832510 has been rounded to 886050111519832448 as a numpy.float.

A hack solution is to use strings, or something more immutable.
```python
    def _common_vocabulary_batch(self, words1, weights1, i2):
        words2, weights2 = self._get_vocabulary(i2)
        words1_np = numpy.array(words1, dtype=numpy.str)
        words2_np = numpy.array(words2, dtype=numpy.str)
        joint, index = numpy.unique(numpy.concatenate((words1_np, words2_np)),
                                    return_index=True)
        nw1 = numpy.zeros(len(joint), dtype=numpy.float32)
        cmp = index < len(words1)
        nw1[numpy.nonzero(cmp)] = weights1[index[cmp]]
        nw2 = numpy.zeros(len(joint), dtype=numpy.float32)
        nw2[numpy.searchsorted(joint, words2)] = weights2
        return joint, nw1, nw2
```

Then convert back to int when querying for the appropriate SpaCy vectors.
```python
# Hook in WMD
class SpacyEmbeddings(object):
    def __getitem__(self, item):
        return nlp.vocab[int(item)].vector
```

issue_title: SPINEL - protocol concerns
issue_body:
some issues with the SPINEL protocol need to be addressed, specifically these deal with power management and sleepy devices.

## Issue 1 - the NCP may be in a deep sleep.

The NCP device may be asleep, or in an ultra low power state and not ready to respond quickly  (immediately) to a host that may be transmitting data.

Typically a GPIO signal from the HOST to wake, or draw the attention of the slave.

The simple approach would be to think the SPI-CS signal is the solution. The reason this fails is many SPI hosts will drop the CS to active, and immediately (within ZERO micro seconds) begin transmitting (clocking) data out of the SPI port. 

Depending upon the sleep state of the NCP and the wakup timing requirements the NCP may not be able to respond, and configure it self for the SPI transfer prior to the host transmitting (or perhaps the UART baudclock source needs to be turned back on)

Examples include:  Interrupt servicing over head at "slow (ie: 32khz) sleep clock rate", length of time to re-enable the on chip oscillator, startup the external crystal and walk the internal power regulators back up to operating voltages.

This can often be made worse when the SPI interface is operating at 10mhz, or higher (vrs the 32khz sleep clock the NCP is waking up from).

The result is the Host & NCP slave can be "off-by-a-bit" or two, or three during the transfer and this problem only occurs rarely... and nobody can figure out why or when this occurs.. maybe the chip wakes up slower when it is very cold (think sensor inside a freezer, or a pool sensor in midwinter)

What is missing is a "NCP-IS-READY" hand shake back to the host.
(total 2 pins)

## Issue 2 How fast can the HOST wake up?

The NCP may need to wake the host up and tell the host there is data to be sent.

This slightly discussed in the SPINEL protocol and repeated here for completeness.

What is not considered is this: A battery powered Linux host might be asleep and in a low power state (example: A cell-phone-data-only device connected to an NCP) the NCP cannot just "raise the request" and start transmitting out the UART... that host might also need to "wake up" and turn clocks on, enable PLLs and so forth before the UART baudrate clock is operational.

Typically another GPIO pair (ncp to HOST) is used to indicate this request.
total: 4 pins.

## Issue 3 - Timing (latency) Problems 

COST PROBLEM - every pin on a chip costs money, or makes a product require a more expensive chip with more pins to provide the solution (this is great for those selling the chip, but bad for buyer of the chip)

A fundamental truth is this:  Digital signals can cross 3 to 4 inches on a circuit board without problems at relatively high frequency. There is a myth that UARTs are not reliable, that was true when the signal ran across 300 feet of wire from one end of a factory to another end of the factory. Here we are dealing with 3 to 4 inches of signal traces on a circuit board or small flex cable. The state of the art is such that you do not have signal quality problems. The truth is often something else. The problem is often the power up sequences (above) or this race condition, or you are writing very in-efficient C code.

In general, one does not want 4 GPIO hand shake pins 

- To solve issue 1  (host -> ncp) requires 2 pins, a request and ack
- To solve issue 2 (ncp -> host) requires 2 pins, a request and ack.
- 

The economic need is this:
    2 pins UART (txd/rxd)  + 2 GPIO handshake = total 4
    3 pins SPI  (mosi,miso,clock)  + 2 GPIO handshake = total 5

Notice that the SPI does not use an actual CS signal - in theory it is present, just not hooked up or the GPIO pin is reconfigured in a "non-SPI-mode" and is used as a GPIO pin instead. 

If the slave (NCP) requires this signal, the HOST_REQACK can be used as the SPI_CS signal. The slave will see the SPI_CS assert, then a seemingly long delay before the SPI_CLK starts.

## Issue 4  - hardware design problem, power management

Sometimes the GPIO signals are also used to enable power to voltage level shifters between different power domains (ie: maybe the HOST uses VDD-IO as 1.2v, but the radio uses 3.3v?) Will enabling the level shift cost another GPIO pin? Or can we reuse one of the HANDSHAKE GPIO signals? The Money answer is simple: An existing GPIO signal needs to be reused reused.

## The Timing Race Problem that occurs because of issues 1, 2, 3 and 4.

The race condition happens because of economizing the number of GPIO signals

The race condition is this:

## At Time 0 - 
Both the Host and the NCP decide "i-need-to-send-data"
Both assert their single GPIO signal at the exact same time.
  
the host is often a linux machine, the user level IRQ latency is horrible compared to the NCP-microcontroller. Latency is defined as:  NCP-GPIO assert, to HOST-GPIO-Ready assert, or further first bit has transferred over the connection.

## The RACE is this:

The host assumes the slave responded "OK-READY" via the GPIO, however the slave is sending "REQUEST_TO_SEND"

Depending upon which packet is "longer" - the other side will need to insert "SPI-STUFFING" values.
(the UART case does not require stuffing values)

## Consider the scenario: 

- The host begins transmitting a very tiny packet.
- The slave begins transmitting a very LARGE packet.
- Note: the opposite can also occur.

## At time HOSTDATA_end

When the host finishes, in the SPI case, the host will "drop the GPIO" or stop the SPI clock
The slave however is still sending data.

## In the UART case

The host can easily tell if the target has sent anything.
do you have bytes in the UART RX buffer? yes or no.
   
IF YES - determine the length of the message.
And if required wait until the end of the message before dropping the handshake pin.

## In the SPI case

Assuming the host (linux) is a SPI-MASTER, the NCP will be the SPI-SLAVE role, the side sending the smaller packet must send "stuffing" values over the wire.

Or - maybe the (the race condition is occurring) the NCP slave is sending a response while handling an incoming message.

Because we limit to only 1 transfer per handshake..

Again, the host needs to examine the first (N) bytes of the SPI transfer and determine
Question 1:  Is the slave sending "stuffing values"
OR question 2: Is the slave sending a packet?

The host needs to examine the partial SPI transfer and determine: Was this stuffing values? or Is this the start of a message?  And if so - how many more SPI clocks are required? Or how long can we wait to turn the power off to the level-shift devices?

Recall that - we only transfer a single message per handshake, and the message will always start at an appropriate place (where the sender REQACK signal was first asserted).  We never start a message in the middle of, or during the transfer of another message. The transfer then becomes some what atomic, due to the REQACK framing that occurs.

## Summary

Only one transfer is performed per "handshake". This means the host must deassert the HOST_REQACK signal at the end of the transfer. (more about this below)

To send (HOST -> NCP)

- GPIO (1) = HOST REQACK, the host asserts this to send data.
- GPIO (2) = SLAVE REQACK, the slave indicates it is ready to receive.

To send (SLAVE -> NCP)

- GPIO (2) = SLAVE REQACK, the slave asserts this indicating it needs to send.
- GPIO (1) = HOST REQACK, the host asserts this indicating it is ready to receive

For UARTS - this can be done with the RTS/CTS signal mapping and various IO/CTL operations
But honestly using the GPIO api is a better solution.

For SPI:

- the host (linux) is always a master (some smartphone chips are really dumb, and cannot operate in slave mode) we can assume these have more resources. 
- SPI requires the SCLK, MOSI, MISO - we cannot avoid these.
- 

The SPI_CS is something we can work around. On the slave side may require the SPI_CS signal to operate correctly. If so - the HOST_REQACK signal can function as a SPI_CS signal.

While the host may generate a SPI_CS - it is not really required or usable (see issues 1 and 2 above) this signal can either be (a) left as a no-connect, or (b) the mode of the HOST_SPI_CS signal could be set to "GPIO mode" and controlled via software.

## FAST TURNAROUND - streaming packets.

Often in the NCP (micro-controller) this is not an issue, within a few microseconds it is ready to go.

The host side, from user space through the kernel and out the GPIO driver is slow. We can use this to our advantage.  

First: Measure latency. 

Write a simple user space 'pulse-the-gpio' application on Linux and measure fastest time an 
The code needs to perform a WRITE pin (X [toggling the pin]), then READ pin (Y). Measure the period of the wave form, this is 2x the fastest speed the board change a GPIO Pin.  Note: Linux (and windows) user space GPIO is rather slow. We will use this to our advantage.

Second: 

Pick 50% of that period - the NCP must meet this timing requirement: From END OF DATA to SLAVE_REQACK de-assert - must be completed in that (50%) time window.

OR - The host must insert a busy wait( as described below )

The host at the end of any transfer:

- step 1 - write the HOST_REQACK pin, 
- step 2 - if required insert a short sleep (often this is not required)
- step 3 - read the SLAVE_REQACK pin
- step 4 - determine: Loop for more data, or wait for another interrupt.
- Note: this technique is often found in UART drivers where the hardware supports a FIFO buffer, you will notice the actual hardware IRQ contains a loop - that drains the fifo. That is effectively what we are doing here, the loop draining the transfer buffers.
- 

Note that a Microcontroller based HOST is often much faster at raw GPIO io then a host.
It does vary greatly due to the architecture of the host chip ( often a 2ghz android device cannot read the GPIO pins at 2ghz... they instead require/use a bus frequency shift to a lower speed IO bus)

```python
network = dropout(network, 0.5)
network = fully_connected(network, 256, activation='relu')
```

I then attempted to fine tune, with these layers:

```python
network = fully_connected(network, 4096, activation='relu', restore=False)
network = dropout(network, 0.5)
network = fully_connected(network, 4096, activation='relu', restore=False)
network = dropout(network, 0.5)
network = fully_connected(network, 256, activation='relu', restore=False)
```

I get this error:
```
tensorflow.python.framework.errors.InvalidArgumentError: Assign requires shapes of both tensors to match. lhs shape= [3136,4096] rhs shape= [3136,1024]
```

It makes sense that the loaded model weights can't be restored onto these newly shaped layers, but I expect that was what `restore=False` would do.  Thank you in advance for the great project and whatever help you can offer.

issue_title: simplifying interface
issue_body:
Hi,
I love sklearn-pandas and it has been extremely useful in helping setup transformation pipeline. However, there are few holes for which I have started a new project that creates another level of wrapper around sklearn-pandas. You can find my package over [here](https://github.com/ragrawal/pandas-transformer). It especially fix these two issues:

1. Simply interface to apply transformation on original or derived columns.
2. Wrap simple functions and transform them into a derived class of TransferMixin. 

I would love to integrate my project with sklearn-pandas itself. Let me know what you guys think about this. 
Ritesh

issue_title: Question about returning unique pointer in virtual function
Casting help / documentation
Docstring for each value of an Enum
issue_body:
## Issue description

I haven't found a way to specify a docstring describing the role of each value of an enum (documentation, FAQ, tests, and `pybind11.h`).

The `value` method seems like a perfect candidate: it could accept an optional docstring similarly to `def`.

## Reproducible example code

```
py::enum_<MyEnum>(m, "MyEnum",
        "This docstring is for the overall enum.",
        py::arithmetic())
        .value("EOne", EOne)  // No way to pass per-value docstring here.
        .value("ETwo", ETwo)
        .export_values();
```

issue_title: left shift of int_
issue_body:
This code:
```
    max (sizeof(out_type) == sizeof(uint64_t) ?
         py::cast(py::int_(1) << 64) : py::cast(py::int_(1) << 32)),
```
produces this warning:
```
pn2.cc:130:31: warning: left shift count >= width of type [-Wshift-count-overflow]
          py::cast(py::int_(1) << 64) : py::cast(py::int_(1) << 32)),
                       ~~~~~~~~^~~~~
```

Clearly this isn't doing what I expected (and I believe worked under bp::int_).  I expected this to produce a python int object, then use the boost::python object protocol to apply the << operator.  I'm confused.

issue_title: Support for managed attachments
issue_body:
I would like to see support for the recently proposed "managed attachements" in vdirsyncer and khal, see the [draf](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-calext-caldav-attachments-01) for details.

From the caldeveloper mailing list I take it, that currently only Apple's client and server, and the CMU/Fastmail Cyrus server support it, but this is obviously a chicken and egg problem somebody will need to break.

So, we could just save all attachments in a new subfolder (e.g. `attachements`), but would need to transform the urls to something we can save on disk.

While we are at it, we could also think about supporting [Time Zones by Reference
](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7809).

I'll see if I can find the time to whip a PR together.

issue_body:
Hi,

This is a small issue with the **certbot rpm** postscript script in the directories creation `/etc/letsencrypt/`.

Directories `archive`and `live` should be read accessible for any user so any daemon can use let's encrypt certificate.

thanks again for this awesome solution !

## My operating system is (include version):

CentOS Linux release 7.4.1708 (Core)

## I installed Certbot with yum from epel repo:

```
[root@web-proxy-001 ~]# rpm -qa|grep certbot
python2-certbot-0.19.0-1.el7.noarch
certbot-0.19.0-1.el7.noarch
```

## I ran this command and it produced this output:

I use the certificates symbolic link `/etc/letsencrypt/live/MyDomain.com/fullchain.pem` and `/etc/letsencrypt/live/MyDomain.com/privkey.pem` from my server but I've got this error:

`load_cert(): TLSs<default>: Unable to load certificate file ...`

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:

Directory Permission on `/etc/letsencrypt/live/`and `/etc/letsencrypt/archive/`

```
 # ll -d /etc/letsencrypt/live/
drwx------. 3 root root 38 Sep 28 11:24 /etc/letsencrypt/live/

# ll -d /etc/letsencrypt/archive/
drwx------. 3 root root 38 Sep 28 11:24 /etc/letsencrypt/archive/
```

## To fix this error:

```
# chmod 755 /etc/letsencrypt/live/
# chmod 755 /etc/letsencrypt/archive/
# ll /etc/letsencrypt/
total 0
drwxr-xr-x. 3 root root 38 Sep 28 11:24 archive
drwxr-xr-x. 3 root root 38 Sep 28 11:24 live
...
```

issue_title: WebUI: Channel Events view misses events
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

The Channel events view in the WebUI often doesn't update with new events.

Steps to reproduce:
- Open a channel 
- Open 'Channel Events' view 
- Initiate a transfer from outside the WebUI (preferably to a partner that will send some tokens back, e.g. the echo node)
- Notice that most of the time the transfer events don't show up

The reason seems to be that the frontend uses a too strict block limit of only the current block when querying for the events. This leads to under-sampling of events if the call isn't performed at exactly the moment before a new block appears (which is unlikely to say the least).

Proposed solution: Use `from_block=<current - 1>` when querying for updates to the event list.

issue_title: Force the opening party to do a deposit
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

A channel is only considered open after the first deposit, this is adding complexity in the code base to handle initializing channels, forcing the party creating the channel to deposit is a nice way to fix this. At the same time, this is a nice optimization, reducing the number of transactions required to open a channel from 2 to 1, and makes more sense, since the party willing to deposit in the channel should be the one creating it, not the other way around.

## Tasklist

- [ ] change the channel manager to also do a token deposit after the channel is created
- [ ] remove the notion of initializing channel from the the code base

## Related

https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/issues/517

issue_title: Harden messages against replay attacks
issue_labels: [{'id': 456350195, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/raiden-network/raiden/labels/dev:%20Please%20Review', 'name': 'dev: Please Review', 'color': '006b75', 'default': False}, {'id': 473091760, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/raiden-network/raiden/labels/low_hanging_fruit', 'name': 'low_hanging_fruit', 'color': 'bfd4f2', 'default': False}, {'id': 473052392, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/raiden-network/raiden/labels/mvp_protocol', 'name': 'mvp_protocol', 'color': 'b60205', 'default': False}, {'id': 473052250, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/raiden-network/raiden/labels/mvp_security', 'name': 'mvp_security', 'color': '5319e7', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Depending on the value of `self.asset`, messages could be re-applicable between two instances of the same state channel `(between=[Alice, Bob], asset=BBCoin)`. Given that:
- Alice and Bob opened+settled channel for BBCoin yesterday AND
- Alice and Bob open a new channel for BBCoin

If `self.asset` resolves to the same value, there seems to be no value unique to the current instance, i.e. yesterdays messages could be valid in today's channel.

So: either the asset-address needs to be unique, or we should include the state-channel address in signed transfer messages.

See:
https://github.com/brainbot-com/raiden/blob/master/raiden/messages.py#L291 
https://github.com/brainbot-com/raiden/blob/master/raiden/messages.py#L394

issue_title: Too many task files slows down SoS
issue_body:
This is separated from #796. There is slowness not only in job preparation but also at the end of every for loop in tasks -- my task queue will always halt for about 10 seconds before submitting the next batch of jobs, ie, the overhead of submitting 1000 jobs is 500 seconds. Today I finally can no longer live with the slowness so I removed my `~/.sos/tasks`. Out of curiosity I `tar zcf` before I delete it. It took quite a while to archive it, and generated for me a 1.9G `tar.gz` file. That might be a 10GB sqlite database if that's what we will end up doing, and growing ... that sounds disturbing.

This may be an important issue for heavy duty work. I'm really curious how other software deal with this problem.

issue_title: Global `task` folder
issue_body: Sorry for submitting many tickets but this one certainly is of low priority (tagged to `later`). The number of `task` files accumulated in `~/.sos` is a bit worrisome to me. I'm wondering how many files do people usually have there? I removed my `~/.sos/task` on Friday morning, and by this time I've got 61072 files in `~/.sos/task`. This is two days working on just one project. I understand the advantages to have global task but I'm worried there will eventually be too many files there. I'm thinking maybe we have to move it to local `.sos`, or an option for it at least; or some way to clean it up when there are too many of them ...

issue_title: Limit maximum download jobs
issue_body:
Same example as #926 but instead of worrying about disk space we worry here the download bandwidth balance. The workflow is again pasted here:

```
[1]
input: for_each = ['files_sra'], group_by = 1, concurrent = True
output: files_sra
download: 
  {url}/{_files_sra}

[2]
output: files_bam
run:
 sratools-dump ${_input} ${_output}
```

Apparently we cannot use very large `-j` for step 1 due to band-width limit. But if we use eg, `-j 1` then after download step 1, the `sratools` decryption step 2 will take long time, and the workflow will not download anything. This is a waste of resource. 

So ideally we want something like this:

```
1st file download -> 1st file decryption &  2nd file download -> 2nd file decryption & 3rd file download ...
```

How can we use SoS existing features to achieve this?
